message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>There is scarce information as to the durability of immune responses elicited by the Comirnaty® COVID-19 vaccine in nursing home residents. Here, we assessed SARS-CoV-2-Spike (S)-targeted antibody and functional T cell responses at around 6 months after complete vaccination.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The sample comprised 46 residents (34 females; age, 60-100 years), of whom 10 had COVID-19 prior to vaccination. Baseline (median of 17.5 days after vaccination) and follow-up (median, 195 days) plasma specimens were available for quantitation of SARS-CoV-2-S antibodies and enumeration of SARS-CoV-2-S-reactive IFN-γ CD4
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    and CD8
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    T cells by flow cytometry.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In total, 44/45 participants had detectable SARS-CoV-2-S antibodies at follow-up. Overall, antibody levels were found to decrease (median, 4.8 fold). Antibodies waning was more frequent (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.001) in SARS-CoV-2 naïve (29/35) than in recovered (1/10) residents. SARS-CoV-2-S IFN-γ CD8
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    T cells were detected in 33/46 and 24/46 at baseline and follow-up, respectively. The figures for CD4
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    T cell counterparts were 12/46 and 30/46. Detectable SARS-CoV-2 IFN-γ CD8
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    and CD4
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    T cell responses at follow-up were more common in recovered (8/10 and 7/10, respectively) than in naïve residents (9/36 and 25/36, respectively). For those with detectable responses at both time points, SARS-CoV-2-S IFN-γ CD8
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    T cell frequencies decreased significantly (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.001) over time whereas the opposite (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.01) was observed in CD4
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    T cells.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Almost all residents displayed detectable SARS-CoV-2-S-reactive antibodies and T cell responses, respectively, by around 6 months after complete vaccination with Comirnaty® COVID-19 vaccine, albeit generally waning in magnitude over time.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Evolution of SARS-CoV-2 immune responses in nursing home residents following full dose of the Comirnaty® COVID-19 vaccine

reference.article-title: Incident SARS-CoV-2 Infection among mRNA-Vaccinated and Unvaccinated Nursing Home Residents

reference.unstructured: Torres I , Albert E , Giménez E , Alcaraz MJ , Botija P , Amat P , et al. B and T cell immune responses elicited by the Comirnaty COVID-19 vaccine in nursing-home residents. Clin Microbiol Infect 2021;S1198-743X(21)00332-3.
